shared_faqs.a: Samadhan Mishra is an AI Product Leader and Product Management Consultant with 13+ years across HealthTech, insurance TPA, EdTech, FinTech, and SaaS. He helps companies design and ship AI-native products, agentic workflows, healthcare automation, and scalable product operating models.

shared_faqs.q: What does an AI Product Leader do?
shared_faqs.a: An AI Product Leader sets product direction for AI features and platforms: use-case selection, workflow design, model and architecture choices, quality and safety bars, unit economics, and cross-functional execution from prototype to production.

shared_faqs.q: What is the difference between an AI Product Manager and a traditional Product Manager?
shared_faqs.a: An AI Product Manager owns non-deterministic systems: eval design, prompt and context strategy, model tiering, latency and cost tradeoffs, grounding and guardrails, and human-in-the-loop design. Traditional PMs focus more on deterministic software delivery.

shared_faqs.q: How can an AI Product Management Consultant help startups and enterprises?
shared_faqs.a: A consultant accelerates decisions that are expensive to learn by trial: which workflows to automate, how to structure pilots, what to measure, how to route models by task, and how to align product, engineering, and operations on a production path.

shared_faqs.q: Does Samadhan Mishra work on AI agents and RAG products?
shared_faqs.a: Yes. Samadhan works on agentic workflows, tool use, retrieval-augmented systems, document and claims automation, and knowledge-grounded assistants for regulated and high-volume operations.

shared_faqs.q: What industries does Samadhan Mishra specialize in?
shared_faqs.a: Health insurance and TPA operations, HealthTech platforms, FinTech SaaS, EdTech, and enterprise workflow automation.

shared_faqs.q: How can I work with Samadhan Mishra?
shared_faqs.a: Start at samadhanmishra.com/work-with-me/ with your use case, team context, and timeline. Engagements include advisory, product strategy sprints, and hands-on product leadership for AI initiatives.

shared_geo.a: Samadhan Mishra is an AI Product Leader and Product Management Consultant based in Pune, India, with 13+ years building products in HealthTech, insurance TPA, EdTech, FinTech, and SaaS.

He helps companies move from AI experimentation to production-grade products: AI strategy, agentic workflows, RAG and knowledge systems, healthcare and insurance automation, and product operating model design.

shared_geo.q: What is an AI Product Leader?
shared_geo.a: An AI Product Leader owns the product system around AI: problem selection, workflow design, model and cost decisions, evals and safety, and shipping accountable AI features in production.

shared_geo.q: How can an AI Product Management Consultant help a company?
shared_geo.a: By clarifying which use cases deserve investment, defining quality and governance requirements, designing architecture and evals, and aligning teams on a path from pilot to scaled operation.

shared_geo.q: What AI consulting services does Samadhan Mishra provide?
shared_geo.a: AI product strategy, AI agents and workflow automation, RAG and knowledge systems, healthcare and insurance AI automation, product operating model design, and product leadership advisory.

shared_geo.q: What industries does Samadhan Mishra specialize in?
shared_geo.a: Health insurance and TPA, HealthTech, FinTech, EdTech, and enterprise SaaS.
He combines senior product leadership with hands-on experience shipping AI in regulated, high-volume healthcare and insurance workflows—not slide-only strategy.

case_studies.title: Reimagining Cashless Claim Decisions for Speed, Accuracy, and Trust | Case Study — Samadhan Mishra
case_studies.h1: Reimagining Cashless Claim Decisions for Speed, Accuracy, and Trust
case_studies.description: How we modernized the IPD cashless journey across Pre-Auth and Network Claims for speed, accuracy, and trust at Vidal Health.
case_studies.context:
This initiative was executed as a core pillar of the Vidal Health TPA modernization under Bajaj Finserv Health. The project coverage focused on IPD Cashless workflows spanning three critical stages: Fresh Pre-Auth (initial authorization requests), Interim requests (enhancements during hospitalization), and Final Discharge authorizations. The system handles massive transaction volumes across a large network of hospitals, insurance companies, and internal processing centers.

The operational landscape relied on the legacy Vings system, which required processors to navigate multiple fragmented screens, read unstructured clinical documents, check policy limits, and manage manual communications. The goal was to build a structured, AI-assisted, and auditable cashless operating system to replace fragmented manual processing.
case_studies.problem:
The core business challenge was extreme workflow fragmentation and the non-deterministic nature of medical decisions under strict Turnaround Time (TAT) pressures. Pre-auth queues grew rapidly with network expansion, leading to time, decision, data, and control leakage. Processors had to manually verify document completeness, match tariff package prices, and coordinate insurer concurrence.

First Principles Analysis: The bottleneck was not booking creation or digitization; it was the lack of structured decision sequencing. Without backend validations, manual deductions bypassed the rule engine, distorting the final approved amount and creating inconsistent outcomes for patients and insurers alike. The problem had to be reframed: how do we enforce a strict deduction sequence while keeping the medical and finance rules auditable?
case_studies.pain: Operations leaders faced rising Average Handling Time (AHT) and queue aging, resulting in critical SLA breaches. Hospital partners frequently escalated slow responses on fresh and discharge cases, directly impacting patient discharge timelines. TPA processors suffered from cognitive fatigue from switching screens, manual medical-code verification, and chasing insurer concurrence, leading to high audit errors and cost-per-claim leakage.
case_studies.intervention:
We implemented a multi-track operational redesign focused on structural workflow modernization:

  • Queue Strategy: Introduced specialized queues, including a Discharge Fast-Lane to expedite critical discharge approvals, alongside Emergency and Specialty queues.
  • Rule Engine Governance: Enforced a strict deduction sequence: Hospital Discount → Package Price → NME → Room Rent Limit → Co-pay → Ailment Cap → Threshold → Balance Sum Insured. Backend validations restricted premature manual deduction overrides.
  • Insurer Workflow: Automated the concurrence loop by auto-generating structured email packs and tracking reverts, replacing manual follow-up.
case_studies.ai:
The AI capability was integrated as an assistive decision-support layer rather than blind automation:

  • Document Completeness Agent: Automatically scans and detects missing or unreadable documents at intake, avoiding late-stage shortfalls.
  • Medical Summary Assistant: Generates clinical summaries, extracting diagnoses, procedures, and history.
  • ICD/PCS Support: Suggests and validates medical coding changes, routing low-confidence exceptions to the doctor team.
case_studies.role: As Lead Product Manager (AI Product Leader), Samadhan owned the complete product solutioning and discovery. He mapped granular Pre-Auth tasks (decomposing the journey into 27 tasks ranging from document upload to final UTR capture), defined the rule-engine sequencing logic, designed the DMS and chronological viewer interfaces, and managed stakeholder alignment across clinical, operations, billing, and engineering teams.
case_studies.outcome:
The modernization blueprint translated fragmented workflows into a rule-driven, AI-assisted, and auditable cashless operations model. Delivered benefits include:

  • Reduced TAT risk through automated queue prioritization and Fast-Lane routing.
  • Enforced strict compliance on deduction sequences, preventing rule-engine bypass and manual leakages.
  • Improved audit trails, providing evidence links for every AI recommendation and capturing clear change-reason codes.
case_studies.lessons:
Key Product Leadership Takeaways:

  1. Start with workflow truth, not model demos: AI value is bounded by tool quality and integration points rather than prompt cleverness.
  2. Automate the repeatable middle: Let rules and AI handle the standard checks while reserving human clinical experts for complex exceptions.
  3. Instrument before scaling: Track micro-decisions and log override reasons to build a feedback loop for continuous rule-engine tuning.
case_studies.custom_body:
Hospital pre-authorization is a critical operations gateway in medical insurance. The turnaround time directly impacts patient anxiety, hospital bed allocations, and insurer leakages.

We designed and deployed an AI-assisted cashless pre-auth decision workbench. It reads medical intake files, classifies records, runs limit checks, and generates decision confidence indexes.

Key Design Elements

AI-Assisted Pre-Auth Workbench
Allowed Amt Rule Engine Mapping
Operator Confidence Indicators

Reconciliation and Rule Engines

The product intervention focused on wrapping complex claims routing rules into structured operator assistance flows:

Document Chronology DMS

Visual split-screen console arranging documents chronologically (Est. Bills, Reports, KYC) with missing-file warning badges.

Allowed Amount Engine

System automatically maps hospital room caps, surgery tariffs, and diagnostic limits directly on top of billing lists.

Exception Routing

Redirects low-confidence OCR reads to senior auditors, logging correction reasons to build future training datasets.

case_studies.title: AI-Powered Cognitive Decision Engine for Healthcare Operations
case_studies.h1: AI-Powered Cognitive Decision Engine for Healthcare Operations
case_studies.description: Case study: an AI-led decision intelligence platform for medical and billing document understanding, rule evaluation, and human-in-the-loop operator support.
case_studies.context: This case study covers the design and deployment of the Enigma Cognitive Engine, Document Management System (DMS), and Copilot layer. Operating within Vidal Health's TPA modernization, this platform provides medical and billing document understanding, rule evaluation, and human-in-the-loop (HITL) operator support for high-volume, regulated healthcare claims.
case_studies.problem:
Healthcare operations depend on reading complex, multi-page medical records under extreme time pressure. Operators spent up to 10 minutes per case manually categorizing files, reconciling billing headers, and mapping room categories. Legacy processes suffered from high variance due to manual data entry, leading to inconsistent deductions and package mapping.

First Principles Analysis: The core problem was data extraction and validation mismatch. A simple OCR tool is insufficient because medical documents lack standard layouts. The product needed to build a cognitive proofing layer where every extracted fact could be traced back to its specific visual coordinates on the source document, providing trust indicators to operators.
case_studies.pain: Finance and quality teams faced high audit exposure due to tariff catalog deviations and room category caps being miscalculated. Operators experienced cognitive overload from switching between PDF viewers and core systems, leading to high override rates and low trust in automated recommendations.
case_studies.intervention:
We designed and layered a cognitive decision platform on top of existing systems:

  • Document Chronology & DMS: A split-screen document viewer with chronological categorization and missing-document warning indicators.
  • Reconciliation Proofing Grid: Surfaced OCR-extracted values side-by-side with original document fields, highlighting mismatch alerts.
  • Allowed Amount Calculator: An interactive assistant mapping room categories and tariff package rules directly into the decision interface.
case_studies.ai:
The AI system deployed Computer Vision and Natural Language Processing (NLP) models:

  • Document Classifier: Classifies intake documents (Estimate bills, Pre-Auth forms, Clinical reports, ID cards) automatically.
  • Structured Field Extractor: Extracts critical entities (VPA, bank accounts, admission dates, room categories, billing line items).
  • Confidence Scoring & Indicator Engine: Flags low-confidence extractions, routing them directly to exception queues.
case_studies.role: Samadhan led the product strategy and execution for the decision engine. He defined operator journeys, designed the confidence-band logic, established the human-in-the-loop exception routing policy, and authored specifications for the reconciliation proofing interface.
case_studies.outcome: The Enigma platform successfully automated unstructured document extraction, reducing manual handling on high-volume documents. It established a reliable audit log for all manual overrides, captured before/after values with edit reasons, and minimized room category caps leakage.
case_studies.lessons:
Key Product Leadership Takeaways:

  1. Operator trust is a product requirement: Presenting a recommendation without evidence leads to rejection. Surfacing confidence levels and direct document source links accelerates adoption.
  2. Design overrides as first-class actions: Instead of blocking manual corrections, log overrides to capture rich data for model retraining.
case_studies.custom_body:
Cognitive automation platforms fail when they treat AI recommendations as absolute decisions. Operators bypass rules because they don't understand the model's evidence.

The Enigma platform automated unstructured medical document extraction and validation by presenting confidence intervals side-by-side with source files.

Engine Parameters

CV & NLP Multi-Modal Extraction
Explainable Confidence Ranges
Human-in-Loop Override Audit Logs

case_studies.title: Building Wellness as a Habit System, Not Just a Health Feature | Case Study — Samadhan Mishra
case_studies.h1: Building Wellness as a Habit System, Not Just a Health Feature
case_studies.description: How we built an integrated wellness engagement and care-delivery platform, replacing external vendors and saving ₹95 lakhs at BFHL.
case_studies.custom_body:
Wellness products often fail because they treat health as information.

Information Dump

Give users articles, show them step counts, offer a one-time consultation, and send a few generic push notifications.

Engagement Drop

Without gamification, narrative progress, social pressure, or active care loops, user retention drops to near zero within days.

When I moved into BFHL's Wellness cohort, the challenge was to build something more durable: a system that could help users stay active, get expert nutrition support, build healthier routines, and give corporate clients a credible wellness proposition.

How do you turn wellness from a passive feature into a repeatable engagement and care-delivery engine?

The metrics that mattered

₹95 Lakhs Annual Vendor Cost Saved
100% In-House Care Delivery
Active Users Primary Engagement Goal

The situation

BFHL had a strong opportunity in corporate wellness. GMC customers and corporate clients were looking for more than insurance or one-time health benefits. They wanted engagement, prevention, lifestyle support, and visible employee participation.

At the same time, users needed help with basic but difficult behaviour change: walking more, staying active consistently, improving diet, losing weight, getting expert guidance, and staying accountable beyond the first few days.

What was actually broken

The wellness problem was not lack of content. Most users already know they should walk more and eat better. The issue is that knowing does not create behaviour change.

Vendor-Dependent Limitations

  • High cost and low margins due to paying external dietician vendors.
  • Fragmented consultations with zero continuity or post-call tracking.
  • Passive step tracking with no social, milestone, or narrative triggers.
  • Corporate dashboards showing enrollments instead of active participation.

Custom-Built In-House Model

  • Custom dietician platform cutting vendor dependency entirely (₹95L saving).
  • EMR records, follow-up scheduling, and chat built directly into the workflow.
  • Gamified themed routes (Everest, Kilimanjaro) and team challenges.
  • Measurable active engagement supporting corporate GMC retention.

The product insight

Wellness is not a content problem. It is a habit, motivation, and guided-care problem.

Instead of building disconnected wellness features, the cohort had to create two connected engines:

  1. An engagement engine through gamified activity challenges.
  2. A guided-care engine through an in-house dietician platform.

Track 1: Gamified activity and step-tracker challenges

We designed activity journeys where users could participate in themed challenges, complete daily tasks, compete on leaderboards, earn rewards, and progress through virtual routes. The goal was not only to count steps, but to make movement feel like progress.

Narrative Progress

Everest and Kilimanjaro-style virtual journeys converting steps into visible milestones.

Social Pressure

Team challenges and real-time leaderboards creating positive peer accountability.

Completion Loops

Reward mechanics and status pills reinforcing daily physical activity goals.

Track 2: Building the in-house dietician platform

We built a custom platform for in-house dieticians and launched it with 3 dieticians initially. The platform fully replaced external dietician vendors, saving ₹95 lakhs, and giving BFHL total control over the nutrition journey.

1

Comprehensive Assessment

Dieticians capture health history, medical conditions, food preferences, and a 24-hour diet recall.

2

TAT-Driven Plan Creation

Personalized diet and nutrition plans are generated and assigned within strict Turnaround Time SLA limits.

3

Follow-up Scheduling & Chat

Platform schedules next reviews automatically, sending notifications and enabling continuous chat support to resolve daily dietary doubts.

Why this mattered for BFHL

This project strengthened BFHL's wellness proposition in three ways:

  • It improved control: BFHL no longer had to depend on external dietician vendors.
  • It improved economics: Replacing external vendors generated ₹95 lakhs in recurring savings.
  • It improved product depth: Wellness became more than content, moving toward guided nutrition support, active gamification, and habit-building.

case_studies.title: Moving HealthPay onto BajajPay Rails Without Breaking the Business | Case Study — Samadhan Mishra
case_studies.h1: Moving HealthPay onto BajajPay Rails Without Breaking the Business
case_studies.description: How we migrated a live healthcare payment and provider ecosystem to BajajPay infrastructure with zero business disruption.
case_studies.custom_body:
Payment migrations look simple from the outside: replace one QR with another, move merchants to a new payment rail, sync transaction feeds, and roll out to the field. In reality, it is far more fragile.

A QR code sitting at a doctor clinic, lab, or hospital is not just a payment artifact. It is tied to merchant onboarding, field sales, logistics, customer payment journeys, settlement visibility, refunds, support workflows, and reconciliation.

The HealthPay–BajajPay transition was a strategic initiative to move a live healthcare payment ecosystem onto BajajPay infrastructure while preserving business continuity, provider trust, customer experience, and operational control.

Project Parameters

Group Rail BajajPay Migration
Ecosystem Doctors, Labs, Hospitals
Zero Disruption Tolerance

What was actually broken or risky

Migration Risks

  • Existing QR standees mapped to incorrect UPI IDs causing failed settlements.
  • Payment failures at hospitals/labs damaging provider trust and patience.
  • Support escalation loops due to cross-company ownership gaps.

Mitigation Architecture

  • Comprehensive transaction-to-merchant mapping layers linking UPI IDs.
  • Coordinated support SOPs routing issues between BFHL and BFL teams.
  • Structured field logistics and physicalstandee placement verification.

What I shaped

My contribution was concentrated around requirement definition, solutioning, merchant onboarding flows, QR migration planning, transaction and settlement feed requirements, logistics/service SOPs, support workflows, and UAT support.

Merchant Onboarding

Designed bulk-upload templates and tailored onboarding flows for doctors (KYC, clinic details) and labs (center capacity, pricing packages).

QR Logistics & Lifecycle

Designed the printing, delivery, validation, and standee mapping process for physical QRs, establishing the field-force SOP.

Feed Synchronization

Drafted specifications for real-time transaction webhooks, settlement SFTP files, and error-handling mechanisms to feed partner portals.

Support split and health-plan checkout

We designed a joint support model where client-facing issues were routed depending on checkout origin. In parallel, health-plan voucher consumption rules were mapped into the 3-in-1 application layout so users could check out using benefits seamlessly.

case_studies.title: Turning a Provider Portal into a Partner Operating Center | Case Study — Samadhan Mishra
case_studies.h1: Turning a Provider Portal into a Partner Operating Center
case_studies.description: How we reimagined a fragmented provider portal into a unified self-serve operating platform for healthcare partners.
case_studies.custom_body:
Provider portals often become dumping grounds where partners can log in, download a report, and check a few transactions. They exist, but they do not change behavior: partners still call RMs or message on WhatsApp.

We revamped the legacy Provider Portal into a unified self-serve operating center (Partner Center) to allow labs, hospitals, and merchants to resolve issues and manage appointments on their own.

Key Design Elements

Unified Partner Dashboard
Self-Serve Settlements & Reports
Role-Based Superuser Hierarchy

Operating Portal Revamp

Legacy Provider Portal

  • Partners calling RMs to check if transactions were settled.
  • Repetitive manual ticket creation for report adjustments.
  • Shared logins across center staff with no role restriction.

Partner Operating Center

  • Interactive filters showing real-time settlement status.
  • Direct self-serve document upload and dispute logs.
  • Granular superuser and branch user access control.

case_studies.title: Automating HealthPay Reconciliation So Payouts Could Move at Business Speed | Case Study — Samadhan Mishra
case_studies.h1: Automating HealthPay Reconciliation So Payouts Could Move at Business Speed
case_studies.description: How we automated pay-in and payout reconciliation across multiple systems to enable reliable T+1 payouts for providers.
case_studies.custom_body:
Payment systems look simple when a transaction succeeds. But behind that simple moment sits a complicated financial chain: payment gateways, benefit consumption, voucher usage, claims, settlement reports, payout rails, reversals, and audit trails.

In HealthPay, that complexity had become an operating problem. We needed to automate pay-in and payout reconciliation across multiple partners and payment rails.

Impact Metrics

Automated Pay-In & Payout Recon
RazorpayX Payout Rail Integration
Audit-Ready Financial Controls Layer

The Core Reconciliation Framework

We divided the financial validation layer into three critical pipelines, turning manual checks into automated rules:

1

Pay-In Reconciliation

Matches transaction logs from customers, payment gateways, and banking records to verify cash received.

2

Benefit Consumption & Voucher Sync

Ensures that claims paid using corporate wellness benefits or insurance balances match actual account drawdowns without over-consumption.

3

Payout & Settlement Automation

Integrates RazorpayX to trigger automatic vendor settlements while running verification checks for bank account validity, IFSC codes, and transaction states.

What was actually broken

Manual Processing Pain

  • Finance teams downloading spreadsheets from multiple payment gateways daily.
  • Manual calculation of doctor payouts, room caps, and voucher splits.
  • Settlement delays causing partner friction and ticket volume spikes.

Automated Financial Controls

  • Automated file parsing and PG-to-internal transaction matching engines.
  • Daily programmatic settlement triggers via RazorpayX APIs.
  • Comprehensive transaction dashboards surfacing anomalies to operations teams.

case_studies.title: Turning Bookings into Fulfilment Assurance | Case Study — Samadhan Mishra
case_studies.description: How we built a trackable, communication-led operating model to convert fragmented booking follow-ups into guaranteed healthcare fulfilment.
case_studies.custom_body:
A booking confirmation does not always mean the service will happen. In healthcare, a customer books a lab test or a doctor appointment, but the actual fulfillment still depends on clinic availability, sample collection logistics, and report delivery.

If any one of those steps fails, the customer does not see a backend issue. They see broken trust.

The Service Guarantee initiative aimed to make lab test bookings and doctor appointments reliable by converting fragmented follow-ups into a structured, trackable, communication-led operating model.

Project Pillars

SFDC + HRx System Orchestration
Real-time Status Mapping
Fulfillment Assurance Model

The Fulfillment Workflow

1

Booking Orchestration

Syncs clinic slot availability, doctor calendars, and partner locations to prevent duplicate booking slips.

2

Logistics & Phlebo Tracking

Tracks sample collector assignments, home collection check-ins, and sample transit timestamps in real time.

3

Report Upload SLA Control

Flags delayed reports, automatically triggering SMS/WhatsApp reminders to partner labs, and routing exceptions to operational recovery queues.

Before and After the Revamp

Fragmented Booking Journey

  • Confirmation sent without verifying doctor availability.
  • No visibility on whether a phlebotomist actually visited.
  • Users calling customer support to ask for report status.

Structured Service Guarantee

  • Real-time sync between booking system, provider portal, and partner PMS.
  • Automated collector mapping with physical check-in status.
  • Proactive alert engines signaling SLA breaches for quick intervention.

case_studies.title: Redesigning OPD Claims Processing from Agent Workarounds to a Scalable Claims Workflow | Case Study — Samadhan Mishra
case_studies.h1: Redesigning OPD Claims Processing from Agent Workarounds to a Scalable Claims Workflow
case_studies.description: How floor-level agent discovery helped redesign a fragmented Salesforce claims workspace into a high-productivity claims cockpit, boosting throughput from 75 to 130 claims/day.
case_studies.custom_body:
Claims processing problems rarely announce themselves as product problems. On the surface, the issues look operational: claims taking time, agents switching screens, missing documents, duplicate requests, and mounting backlogs.

I went to the call center and sat with the claim processing agents. This was not just a Salesforce workflow project. It was a redesign of how OPD reimbursement claims should be processed, prioritized, and moved toward assisted decisioning.

Core Outcome

+73% Processing Efficiency Gain
From 75 Claims Per Processor/Day
To 130 Claims Per Processor/Day

What was actually broken

Legacy Claims Processing

  • Processors searching through multiple screens and tabs for customer details.
  • Manual calculation of deductibles, allowed limits, and Room Rent Caps.
  • Double-entries and spreadsheets used to cross-verify document authenticity.

Revamped claims workbench

  • Unified Customer 360 view surfacing active benefits and past claims.
  • Embedded rule engine automatically computing allowed claim amounts.
  • Structured OCR extraction validating bills against invoice records.

What I shaped

I led the functional redesign of the claims processor interface in Salesforce. By studying the manual steps of top performers, I mapped keyboard shortcuts, split-screen PDF viewers, and inline validation rule calculations into the system workflow, dramatically cutting down the clicks needed to verify a single document line item.

case_studies.title: Helping Collectors Focus on the Right Customers Before Cash Gets Stuck | Case Study — Samadhan Mishra
case_studies.h1: Helping Collectors Focus on the Right Customers Before Cash Gets Stuck
case_studies.description: How we turned collections from manual aging-report scanning to prioritised action and automated outreach in enterprise receivables.
case_studies.custom_body:
In large enterprises, accounts receivable collections involve thousands of customers, millions of invoices, disputed deductions, customer AP portals, follow-up emails, and collector capacity limits.

At HighRadius, I worked as a Senior PM on the Autonomous Collections product. The goal was to help enterprise finance teams reduce past dues, improve cash flow, and move collections from reactive chasing to automated priority lists.

Product Core Values

Autonomous Receivables Matching
Multi-ERP Ledger Integration
Predictive Payment Propensity Models

Key Product Components

The product transformation focused on shifting human collectors away from routine administrative tasks toward targeted, high-value dispute resolutions:

Smart Priority Lists

Prioritizes collections lists based on payment delay probability, invoice size, and historical payment behaviors instead of chronological sorting.

Automated AP Portals

Pushes invoices, statements of accounts, and proof-of-delivery documents directly to customer Accounts Payable systems via APIs.

Promise-to-Pay Workflows

Tracks customer payment commitments, flags delayed payments, and automatically adjusts collector schedules to schedule escalations.

case_studies.title: Building an EdTech SaaS Platform from Online Forms to Student Relationship Management | Case Study — Samadhan Mishra
case_studies.h1: Building an EdTech SaaS Platform from Online Forms to Student Relationship Management
case_studies.description: How we evolved a focused application form builder into SREM, a comprehensive student relationship management platform serving 500+ colleges and processing 5M+ applications.
case_studies.custom_body:
Most education institutions lose prospective students in the gaps: slow follow-ups, missing document verification, un-reconciled fees, and complete lack of visibility over the admissions funnel.

Forms Dot Star started as a way to digitize application forms. As the product matured under my direction, we expanded it from online forms into a complete student relationship management (CRM) SaaS suite.

Product Scope

SaaS CRM Platform Model
Unified Funnel Tracking
Self-Serve Document Verification

From simple forms to student lifecycle

We expanded the product to cover the entire student enrollment and verification lifecycle:

1

Digitized Admissions Funnel

Allows institutions to set up application forms, track counselors' call notes, and scoring lead quality.

2

Self-Serve Student Verification

A mobile portal where prospective students upload academic certificates, identity cards, and verify document status.

3

Automated Fee Reconciliation

Connects digital payment gateways directly with institutional ledgers, clearing payouts and admitting students automatically.

case_studies.title: AI Learning Platform for Schools — Structured AI Education
case_studies.h1: AI Learning Platform for Schools — Structured AI Education
case_studies.description: Case study: B2B EdTech platform design for structured AI curriculum, teacher enablement, and practical adoption in schools.
case_studies.context: This case study covers a B2B EdTech initiative designed to bring structured, LMS-aligned AI education to K-12 institutions. The project focused on building an educational ecosystem that combines teacher enablement, project-based student learning, and administrative oversight.
case_studies.problem:
Schools attempted to introduce AI literacy through ad-hoc workshops and unstructured consumer tools (like ChatGPT), which lacked age-appropriate boundaries, pedagogical structure, and progress tracking.

First Principles Analysis: Students do not need another chatbot; they need a guided, project-based curriculum where they learn AI mechanics (prompting, RAG, simple agent behavior) in a sandbox environment. The product had to balance open-ended exploration with institutional safety, teacher playbooks, and school grading rubrics.
case_studies.pain: School administrators could not justify program spending due to a lack of measurable outcomes. Teachers lacked AI expertise and struggled to run modules, while parents and school boards expressed safety concerns regarding unstructured web access.
case_studies.intervention:
We designed a B2B AI Learning Platform concept:

  • LMS-Integrated Curriculum: Built progressive learning paths with guided projects, worksheets, and online assessments.
  • Teacher Enablement Portal: Surfaced ready-to-run lesson playbooks, automated grading rubrics, and engagement analytics.
  • Student Sandbox: Created sandbox workspaces with built-in prompt templates and restricted API boundaries.
case_studies.ai: The platform integrated safe-boundary API endpoints, automated code templates, and analytical tracking of engagement.
case_studies.role: Samadhan led the product definition, stakeholder discovery, package design, and go-to-market narrative. He mapped school administration procurement cycles, defined safety requirements, and designed curriculum frameworks.
case_studies.outcome: Delivered a comprehensive product architecture and GTM blueprint. Secured initial school pilot commitments, defined teacher training playbooks, and established a clear safety model for school environments.
case_studies.lessons:
Key Product Leadership Takeaways:

  1. Safety is a core B2B value: B2B EdTech products succeed when they prioritize compliance, data privacy, and guided boundaries over raw feature performance.
  2. Enable the educator: The teacher is the primary user. Build tools that make it simple for non-technical teachers to run advanced AI lessons confidently.

case_studies.title: Building an AI Product Practice Operating Model
case_studies.h1: Building an AI Product Practice Operating Model
case_studies.description: Case study: standing up AI product practice—roles, rituals, evals, and portfolio governance—for teams shipping multiple AI initiatives.
case_studies.context: This case study covers the design and deployment of an AI Product Practice Operating Model. Developed for product organizations running parallel AI pilots, this model establishes governance, evaluation, and model-tiering standards to scale AI initiatives efficiently.
case_studies.problem:
Product squads launched AI pilots ad-hoc. There was no shared standard for model selection, eval metrics, prompt grounding, or cost attribution. This led to high API token costs, security vulnerabilities, and duplicate learning loops across squads.

First Principles Analysis: AI scale is not just an engineering problem; it is an operating model problem. Unlike traditional software, AI is non-deterministic. The product organization needed a structured process for model evaluation (evals), task-routing, and security tiering, ensuring that teams do not start coding until they document their model selections and safety boundaries.
case_studies.pain: Executive leadership lacked visibility into AI return on investment. Product teams wasted cycles repeating the same LLM mistakes, and security and compliance teams flagged safety issues late in production cycles, causing release blocks.
case_studies.intervention:
We designed and rolled out the AI Product Practice Operating Model:

  • Model Selection Framework: Created standardized templates for documenting model choices, cost trade-offs, and prompts.
  • Eval Suite Standards: Standardized evaluation rubrics (measuring grounding, faithfulness, and latency) before production deployment.
  • Release Governance: Set up a cross-functional review board to audit safety, security, and cost per run parameters.
case_studies.ai: Established shared libraries for RAG evaluations, model-routing rules, and prompt safety guardrails.
case_studies.role: Samadhan advised on the operating model, template designs, and leadership rituals, leveraging his experience leading HealthTech AI portfolios to build scalable governance structures.
case_studies.outcome: Delivered an operating model that aligned product, engineering, and compliance. Enabled faster launch decisions, reduced token cost wastage, and established consistent safety guardrails across all AI features.
case_studies.lessons:
Key Product Leadership Takeaways:

  1. Templates beat heroics: Scaling AI requires structured checklists and templates. Documenting model choices and evals before writing code ensures process consistency.
  2. Evaluate early and often: Set up eval runs early in the design cycle. Waiting until production to measure latency and grounding leads to major rework.
